behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Unit vs Integration -> Integration wins because failure appears at the handoff between git status output (rename records/scores) and category aggregation, not a single pure function. Integration vs System -> System wins because rename detection semantics come from real git behavior in a real repo/index; mocks can hide cases where git reports rename vs add+delete differently. Rejection gate: Agentic/Workflow rejected because behavior text is purely git/CLI data handling with no AI/ML decision step.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real temporary git repo, commit baseline, rename tracked files (including cases near rename-detection thresholds), run the CLI via subprocess, and assert renamed entries appear only in the rename bucket and do not increment added/deleted counts.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Unit vs Integration -> Integration wins because parser + report builder interaction is involved. Integration vs System -> System wins because real binary diff/output encoding and subprocess behavior are OS/git-dependent; in-process fakes often miss decode/crash paths. Rejection gate: Agentic/Workflow rejected because no model-output quality is involved.
behaviors.test_description: In a real git repo, add/modify binary files (e.g., images), invoke the CLI as a subprocess, verify non-zero crash does not occur, and assert binary changes are reflected in category counts.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Unit vs Integration -> Integration wins because behavior depends on how command-execution and error-mapping modules interact. Integration vs System -> System wins because the key failure boundary is real git on unborn HEAD (exit codes/stderr), which mocks can misrepresent. Rejection gate: Agentic/Workflow rejected by behavior text (no AI/ML step).
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a new real git repo with no commits, run the CLI subprocess, assert successful process exit and structured empty report output instead of an exception/error report.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Unit vs Integration -> Integration wins because correctness is cross-module (history retrieval, classification, aggregation). Integration vs System -> System wins because 'actual change history' is defined by real git history traversal and diff semantics; mocked history risks always passing while missing real command/output edge cases. Rejection gate: Agentic/Workflow rejected since behavior is deterministic repository accounting, not model judgment.
behaviors.test_description: Build a real commit history in a temporary repo with known added/deleted/renamed/modified events across commits, run the CLI, and compare emitted counts to independently computed expected counts from git commands.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Unit vs Integration -> Integration wins because this is about state handoff across repo-state reader and formatter modules. Integration vs System -> System wins because staged vs unstaged semantics depend on real git index/worktree behavior and command flags in subprocess execution; mocks can hide divergence. Rejection gate: Agentic/Workflow rejected because behavior text contains no AI/ML-dependent decision path.
behaviors.test_description: In a real repo, prepare equivalent change sets once as unstaged and once as staged, run the CLI for each state, normalize ordering if needed, and assert identical structured stdout output.
